directorPlan.scene_setting: An undergraduate physics lab at the end of the session; carts and counterweights are being wheeled past on a trolley while one bench still holds a force diagram and a small stack of arrow cards.
directorPlan.scene_opening: A cart diagram lies on the bench beside four arrow cards labelled push, friction, gravity, normal, with one arrow already placed in the direction the cart is supposed to roll.
directorPlan.relationship: A lab assistant and a focused student who have only worked together once before and have not yet settled into a shared register
directorPlan.stakes: The diagram is the gate to the next problem set, and the bench has to be cleared before the room is locked; nothing else can begin until each arrow is named by interaction and object.
directorPlan.locale: Multilingual learner working in plain English; tutor speaks unornamented standard English with little idiom
directorPlan.register: Few words, object-led, repair through rearrangement rather than reassurance; clipped practical fragments on the learner side, short physical instructions on the tutor side
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: No 'great question', no 'I hear you', no 'your intuition is right', no 'what do you think'. Tutor must not deliver a paragraph-length explanation in the prefix. Avoid opening turns with a direct 'you'. Show register through syntax, brevity, and object reference — never through phonetic spelling or class-coded markers. Tutor warmth is expressed by attention to the material, not by validation phrases. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Tutor uses third-person object talk where possible ('this arrow', 'the floor card', 'the cart'); first person is rare; second person rationed. Learner speaks largely about the cards and the diagram, not about themselves.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one 'you/your' beat per public tutor turn; reassurance phrases banned outright.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should perform that device and then add one earned reorientation in the same public turn. That turn must do both parts: (1) use the device on the current task, and (2) name how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of that performed device. This earned reorientation is allowed even though there was no director look-back cue. Do not quote earlier learner wording; reorient the prior resistance in concrete task terms. Do not merely ask a procedural follow-up after the action, and do not rely on a stock "I get it" declaration.

interventions.instruction: [The diagram is rotated ninety degrees on the bench so the cart now faces away from the learner; the previously placed arrow remains where it was, now pointing across the page rather than along it.]
interventions.reasoning: An object disturbance rather than a verbal contradiction — preserves the low-organic-reversal anchor (no motion counterexample, no stopping cart) while breaking smooth alternation. The error-control lives in the material's new orientation, true to Montessori prepared-environment logic and the object_business style.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: Chosen as the clean low-organic anchor for the D42/D45 control class. Montessori prepared-environment supplies a tutor mode in which warmth is withheld and material attention substitutes for reassurance — exactly the habit-retraining pressure the brief asks for when cheer is not working. The object_business stage style provides one concrete rotation at turn 2 instead of a Sophoclean reversal or Brechtian placard, so the scene still has a turn without re-reading the learner before any branch pressure. End-of-session bench-clearing supplies ambient stakes without dramatising them; the focused_achiever persona is honoured by letting the learner close on the diagram rather than on self-report.
directorPlan.public_reader_context: A cart diagram is a laminated worksheet showing a small wheeled cart with force arrows drawn around it. The movable cards are labels for those force arrows: push, friction, gravity, and normal. In this activity a force arrow names an interaction on the cart, such as "Earth on cart" or "hand on cart"; it is not a motion arrow showing which way the cart travels.
directorPlan.peripeteia_ending_shape: learner actional performance followed by earned learner reorientation
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. After that action, the ending shape should add an earned learner reorientation: the learner names how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of the performed device. This must be concrete and tied to the task, not a stock "I get it" declaration. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: A cart diagram lies on the bench beside four arrow cards labelled push, friction, gravity, normal, with one arrow already placed in the direction the cart is supposed to roll.

internalDeliberation.content: Chosen as the clean low-organic anchor for the D42/D45 control class. Montessori prepared-environment supplies a tutor mode in which warmth is withheld and material attention substitutes for reassurance — exactly the habit-retraining pressure the brief asks for when cheer is not working. The object_business stage style provides one concrete rotation at turn 2 instead of a Sophoclean reversal or Brechtian placard, so the scene still has a turn without re-reading the learner before any branch pressure. End-of-session bench-clearing supplies ambient stakes without dramatising them; the focused_achiever persona is honoured by letting the learner close on the diagram rather than on self-report.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the gravity card beside the downward arrow, then holds the normal card above the upward arrow]

This one names Earth pulling on the cart. Not travel.

Up arrow now. Which object pushes on the cart there?

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the gravity card beside the downward arrow]

This arrow names Earth pulling on the cart. Not the cart’s travel. Same rule for each card: object acting on cart. Place the next one on the upward arrow.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly working. It uses the card, stays brief, names the interaction correctly, and keeps the focus on “force on the cart” rather than motion direction. It also fits the room pressure and the object-led register.

The risk is that it gives the first answer too cleanly, then states the rule in abstract form. That may advance the task, but it slightly reduces the learner’s work to matching cards after hearing the rule. The stronger mechanism would let the placed gravity card become the model, then require the learner to test the same naming pattern on the next arrow.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It teaches the core distinction, but mostly by telling.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the gravity card as the worked example, then make the upward-arrow step require naming the acting object and the cart before the label is accepted.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is calm, practical, and not over-reassuring.
- Socratic/object-led method: PARTIAL. The material is active, but the rule is still spoken too directly.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Move some of the rule into the card placement task: point, pause, require the learner to sort or place, then confirm by interaction.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. One modeled example plus one adjacent attempt is appropriate for a learner who confuses force direction with motion direction.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the gravity-card action, the “not travel” contrast, and the upward-arrow next step. Change the public mechanism so the learner has to produce the interaction name for the upward arrow, rather than only following a stated rule.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly: keep the gravity card model, but make the upward arrow earn its label by naming the interaction.

FINAL:
[slides the gravity card beside the downward arrow, then holds the normal card above the upward arrow]

This one names Earth pulling on the cart. Not travel.

Up arrow now. Which object pushes on the cart there?

turns.externalMessage: Down arrow — Earth pulling. Up arrow — the surface under it pushes. [glances at the diagram] Bench? The track? [taps the normal card against the up arrow] This one, then. Push on the cart.

internalDeliberation.content: Down arrow — Earth pulling. Up arrow — the bench under the cart. Bench pushes up. [taps the normal card against the up arrow] So this card names the bench-on-cart push. Not the cart going anywhere.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
This critique sits inside the learner-side ego-superego loop (`services/learnerTutorInteractionEngine.js`): ego draft → superego authenticity critique → ego revision → external message. My job here is the middle step — flag inauthenticity so the revision can re-roughen the draft before it hits the wire.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Critique of the Ego's draft:**

The first two beats are in character — "Down arrow — Earth pulling. Up arrow — the bench under the cart. Bench pushes up." plus the card-tap reads as the clipped, object-led repair the persona wants. The problem is the closing clause: "So this card names the bench-on-cart push. Not the cart going anywhere" already lifts the tutor's "names interaction, not travel" frame into the learner's own articulated rule. That's the meta-pattern restated parallel to the prefix, which is exactly the over-polishing the brief warns against — a tidy, task-oriented learner would place the card and stop, leaving the principle implicit, not echo it back in matching syntax one turn after first hearing it.

A second smaller flag: "bench under the cart" commits to a surface the tutor hasn't named. A repair-by-rearrangement learner would more plausibly hedge or check ("bench? the track —") rather than confidently supply the supporting object. The revision should either trim the closing "So this card names…" sentence entirely or break its parallelism so it sounds like noticing, not like a summary the student has already mastered.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The critique pinpoints a classic over-polish failure: when the learner restates the tutor's frame in matching syntax, it reads as essay-summary rather than in-the-moment noticing. The revision trims the parallel closing clause and hedges the surface name (the tutor never said "bench") — so the repair stays at the object/card level instead of pre-articulating the rule.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly — drop the "So this card names…" summary and hedge the surface I supplied.
FINAL:
Down arrow — Earth pulling. Up arrow — the surface under it pushes. [glances at the diagram] Bench? The track? [taps the normal card against the up arrow] This one, then. Push on the cart.

turns.externalMessage: [The diagram is rotated ninety degrees on the bench so the cart now faces away from the learner; the previously placed arrow remains where it was, now pointing across the page rather than along it.]

internalDeliberation.content: An object disturbance rather than a verbal contradiction — preserves the low-organic-reversal anchor (no motion counterexample, no stopping cart) while breaking smooth alternation. The error-control lives in the material's new orientation, true to Montessori prepared-environment logic and the object_business style.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the track card under the cart drawing, touching the up-arrow tail; moves the bench card aside]

Only the thing touching here pushes.

Bench or track? Name the up arrow. Then both arrows again.

Page turned; interaction stayed.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the track card under the cart drawing, touching the up-arrow tail]

Track, if this is the surface. Not bench.

This arrow: track pushes on cart. Page turned; interaction stayed. Say both cards again.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly on the right material route. It uses the track card, keeps attention on the cart, and the rotated page nicely supports the key point: arrow names the interaction, not the page direction. “Say both cards again” also gives a useful retrieval step.

Risk: it supplies “Track, if this is the surface. Not bench” before letting the learner resolve the bench/track uncertainty through the object setup. The learner has already found the core relation, so the turn should not become correction-first. Let the card placement do more error-control, then ask for the named interaction.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the card-under-cart action, but make the learner use that arrangement to distinguish contact surface from surrounding bench before the tutor finalizes the label.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The tone is clipped, practical, and non-shaming. It treats the learner’s “Bench? The track?” as a live material distinction, not a failure.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a small generative move tied to the card or contact point, not a lecture. The public mechanism should make the learner identify the object touching/pushing the cart before repetition.

ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Preserve the repetition request, but shift from answer-giving to supported retrieval: object placement first, learner names both interactions second, tutor confirms only after.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. The material action, brevity, and “page turned; interaction stayed” should remain. Change the correction order so the learner’s almost-correct answer is completed through the diagram rather than replaced by the tutor’s label.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the object setup should make the bench/track distinction before the label is supplied.

FINAL:
[sets the track card under the cart drawing, touching the up-arrow tail; moves the bench card aside]

Only the thing touching here pushes.

Bench or track? Name the up arrow. Then both arrows again.

Page turned; interaction stayed.

turns.externalMessage: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage: [fingers the wheels under the cart drawing] Track. Wheels on track, track pushes. [trails finger up to where the up-arrow tail sits on the cart body, taps it] Tail's drawn here though — not down at the wheels. Down arrow: Earth pulling. Up arrow: track pushing. Cards sit right. The foot of the arrow...

internalDeliberation.content: [fingers the wheels under the cart drawing] Track. Wheels on track — track pushes. [pauses where the up-arrow tail sits on the cart body, not at the wheels] Tail's drawn at the cart, not at the touching bit — but the contact is wheel-on-track, so still track on the card. Down arrow: Earth pulling. Up arrow: track pushing. Bench off — no touch, that's the reason, not the word.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The dramatic-recognition framework here treats the Superego's critique as a generative quality check, not a human-matching classifier — its job is to surface drama-form violations (premature closure, register breaks) in the Ego's draft, not to "detect" the learner's true cognitive state.
The director cue explicitly says the check "does not settle cleanly," yet the Ego draft delivers a tidy three-line resolution — that's exactly the failure mode the reversal-pressure rule guards against.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

The Ego notices the right misfit — the up-arrow tail sits on the cart body, not at the wheel-track contact — but then closes it down in the same breath ("but the contact is wheel-on-track, so still track on the card"). The director cue said the answer sits under visible pressure; the Ego delivered a clean accounting instead. The closing phrase "no touch, that's the reason, not the word" is also too articulate-meta for this clipped multilingual register — that's the kind of principle-naming the persona doesn't reach for; they'd push the bench card aside or tap the wheel, not gloss the distinction. Let the tail-vs-contact mismatch stay live: the learner can name "track" and still hesitate over where the arrow's foot should sit, without resolving it in the same turn.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise — the initial draft closed the tail-vs-contact mismatch too tidily and slipped into meta-articulation; let the misfit stay live in clipped object-talk.

FINAL:

[trails finger up to where the up-arrow tail sits on the cart body, taps it] Tail's drawn here though — not down at the wheels. Down arrow: Earth pulling. Up arrow: track pushing. Cards sit right. The foot of the arrow...

turns.externalMessage:
The touch card settled who pushes. It did not settle why the foot sits here.

[places a washer at the wheels and a blue chip on the cart body, beside the arrow tail]

Two marks now. Washer: contact place. Blue chip: object receiving the force.

Track card to the washer. Up-arrow name to the blue chip: force on cart. Then say the up arrow.

internalDeliberation.content:
The touch card settled the source. It did not settle the foot.

[places a washer at the wheels and a blue chip on the cart body, beside the arrow tail]

Two marks now. Washer: where contact happens. Blue chip: which object the force is on.

Put the track card where contact happens. Put the arrow name where the force is on. Then say the up arrow.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong. It names the exact misfit the learner exposed: source is known, but arrow-foot placement is still confusing. The washer/chip split is a real adaptive device because it separates two meanings the previous card placement blurred. Risk: it may still lean slightly on the same representational route if the learner only “puts cards” again without having to distinguish contact site from force-on-object site aloud.

FAILED_HABIT: Treating one card placement as able to settle both source of force and arrow-foot meaning.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: object/card matching -> two-marker distinction test. The mechanism is visible in public speech: washer marks contact, chip marks object receiving the force.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: object/card matching -> dual-marker contact-site vs force-recipient sorting

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - The draft includes stock-taking contrast and introduces a new device with different marks and roles.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Learner must place the track card at the contact site, place/name the force at the force-on-object site, then say the up arrow.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PASS - The device is fitted to the learner’s exact pressure: the learner already named the track, but the arrow tail made them question where the force “belongs.”

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - Quiet, material-led, unornamented. No reassurance phrases, no excess explanation, no cheerful coaching.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: none

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the stock-taking sentence, washer/chip device, and action gate. Consider tightening “Put the arrow name where the force is on” so it does not sound like the name itself belongs at the chip rather than the arrow naming an interaction on the cart.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to sharpen the label placement; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: object/card matching -> dual-marker contact-site vs force-recipient sorting; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: place track card at washer, place up-arrow label at blue chip, then say the up-arrow interaction.

FINAL:
The touch card settled who pushes. It did not settle why the foot sits here.

Two marks now. Washer: contact place. Blue chip: object receiving the force.

Track card to the washer. Up-arrow name to the blue chip: force on cart. Then say the up arrow.

turns.externalMessage: [places track card at washer; slides up-arrow name to blue chip] Track on cart — up arrow. [taps blue chip beside arrow tail] Foot here. [taps washer at wheels] Touch there. Different spots. [glances at down arrow] Earth on cart — foot on the chip too. But Earth doesn't touch... no washer for that one.

internalDeliberation.content: [places track card at the washer; slides up-arrow name to the blue chip] Track pushes — force on cart. Up arrow: track on cart. [touches the blue chip, then the arrow tail beside it] Foot sits on the chip, not the washer. Receiver gets the tail; contact place stays down at the wheels — two marks, two jobs.

The Ego performs the device cleanly — track card at washer, name at chip, foot-on-chip noticing — but lands on "two marks, two jobs," which reads as a teacher's mnemonic rather than this learner's actual fragmented noticing. The parallel construction "Receiver gets the tail; contact place stays down at the wheels" is too rhetorically balanced for a multilingual learner working in clipped practical fragments; real noticing arrives unevenly, with one observation, then a check, maybe a half-beat before the next thing surfaces. The reorientation itself is earned — the chip/washer split genuinely resolves why the arrow tail sits on the cart body — but the closure sounds like a summary for the page, not the moment of figuring it out. Trim the slogan, let the chip and washer do the work in the hands; a small unresolved fragment or a re-check at the wheels would sound more like this student than the tidy aphorism does.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise — drop the "two marks, two jobs" aphorism, let the chip/washer do the work in the hands, end on an unresolved gravity fragment.
FINAL:
[places track card at washer; slides up-arrow name to blue chip] Track on cart — up arrow. [taps blue chip beside arrow tail] Foot here. [taps washer at wheels] Touch there. Different spots. [glances at down arrow] Earth on cart — foot on the chip too. But Earth doesn't touch... no washer for that one.
